explanation_md: Each call to `show` creates a fresh local variable `n` initialized to `5`, then adds 3 to get `8`, then prints it. When `show` returns, `n` is destroyed. The second call starts over with a new `n = 5`. There is no persistence between calls, so both calls print `8`.

prompt_md: A variable declared inside a function body is destroyed when:

options.c: Execution reaches the closing `}` of the block it was declared in.

explanation_md: Local variables have automatic storage duration: they are created when their declaration is reached and destroyed when execution exits the surrounding block (the matching `}`). The program ending, the value changing, or another function being called have no effect on the lifetime of a local variable.

`f` and `g` each have their own local `x`. They do not share state. `f()` prints its own `x = 1`, `g()` prints its own `x = 2`, and the second `f()` again creates a fresh `x = 1`. Three calls produce three independent outputs.

options.a: `result` is destroyed when `compute` returns, so it is out of scope in `main`.
options.b: You cannot declare variables inside non-`main` functions.
options.c: `result` is a reserved keyword in C++.
options.d: The value 42 is too large to be an `int`.

explanation_md: `result` is local to `compute`. When `compute` returns, `result` is destroyed and its name is no longer in scope. `main` has no knowledge of `result`, so the compiler reports an undeclared identifier error. To share the value, `compute` should return it and `main` should capture the return value.
